coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots incorporated into mesoporous TiO2 films for quantum dot-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Jo, In-Rok
Lee, Young-Hoon
Kim, Hyunsoo
Ahn, Kwang-Soon
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) synthesized by a hydrothermal method were incorporated into TiO2 mesoporous films to boost the photovoltaic performance of quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs). Cadmium selenide quantum dots (CdSe QDs) were then deposited on the N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 films using a successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) technique. The N-GQDs serve multiple functions for enhancing light-harvesting, facilitating electron transportation, and suppressing charge recombination. The non-zero gap N-GQDs both generate excitons as co-sensitizer and provide more adsorption sites for CdSe QDs. Furthermore, the N-GQDs provide efficient electron pathways between TiO2 nanoparticles and passivate the surface defects, giving rise to a high electron diffusion coefficient and long electron lifetime. The resulting QDSSC with the N-GQDs showed significantly improved power conversion efficiency (4.88%) compared to the QDSSC (3.92%) without N-GQDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of N-GQDs and (b) high-resolution TEM image of N-GQD. (c) The corresponding size distribution of N-GQDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of graphene oxide (GO) and N-GQD. (e) Raman spectra and ID/IG ratio of GQDs and N-GQDs. (f) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra at different excitation wavelengths and UV–vis absorption spectra of the N-GQDs aqueous solution. The inset is an optical image of an N-GQDs solution excited at 365 nm.
(a) Cross-sectional SEM image of FTO/TiO2/N-GQDs electrode and corresponding EDS elemental mapping images of (b) Ti, (c) C, and (d) O, respectively. (e) EDS spectrum.
XPS spectra of N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 film. (a) Survey scan spectrum, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of TiO2/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) and N-GQDs-modified photoanodes with different CdSe SILAR cycles. (b) Corresponding Tauc plots from the absorption spectra.
J–V curves of the QDSSCs with unmodified TiO2/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) and TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (6, 7, 8, and 9 cycles) photoanodes.

Comparison of the electronic characteristics of photoanodes obtained by the PLIM measurement. (a) Electron diffusion coefficients, (b) electron lifetimes, (c) charge collection efficiencies, and (d) diffusion lengths of the TiO2/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) and TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (8 cycles), respectively.
Nyquist plots of the QDSSCs measured at Voc under 1sun illumination.
Mott-Schottky plots of bare TiO2 and the N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 electrodes measured at 1 kHz in 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution in the dark.
XPS parameters of TiO2/N-GQDs film determined from curve fitting. The relative sensitivity factor (R.S.F) values were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific.
Optical bandgap energies calculated from UV–vis absorption spectra.

Photovoltaic performances obtained from the J-V curves of the QDSSCs with different photoanodes and Cu2S CEs. The values in parenthesis indicate the average and the standard deviation of three independent cells.
EIS parameters obtained from Nyquist plots of QDSSCs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) synthesized by a hydrothermal method were incorporated into TiO2 mesoporous films to boost the photovoltaic performance of quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs). Cadmium selenide quantum dots (CdSe QDs) were then deposited on the N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 films using a successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) technique. The N-GQDs serve multiple functions for enhancing light-harvesting, facilitating electron transportation, and suppressing charge recombination. The non-zero gap N-GQDs both generate excitons as co-sensitizer and provide more adsorption sites for CdSe QDs. Furthermore, the N-GQDs provide efficient electron pathways between TiO2 nanoparticles and passivate the surface defects, giving rise to a high electron diffusion coefficient and long electron lifetime. The resulting QDSSC with the N-GQDs showed significantly improved power conversion efficiency (4.88%) compared to the QDSSC (3.92%) without N-GQDs.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction

ce:section.ce:para: Since the industrial revolution, the consumption of energy based on fossil fuels has increased rapidly [1]. The ever-increasing emissions of CO2 from various industrial processes are causing serious concerns regarding climate change. Recently, renewable and eco-friendly energy sources, such as solar, wind, and hydropower, have been required to meet these demands [2]. Among them, solar energy has attracted considerable attention as a continuous and independent source for power generation [3]. Quantum dot-sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs), the third-generation solar cell in photovoltaic systems, have been studied intensively because of the expectation that they can exceed the maximum theoretical efficiency, called the Shockley-Queisser limit, via multiple exciton generation (MEG) [4,5]. These cells use quantum dots (QDs), which have a size-dependent bandgap, as a photo-sensitizer.

Typically, QDSSCs are composed of a mesoporous TiO2 photoanode, in which the QD films are deposited, a polysulfide (S2-/Sn 2-) redox couple, and a counter electrode (CE) [6–8]. Considerable efforts have been made to improve the photovoltaic performance of QDSSCs, such as enhancing light-harvesting by adopting new QDs [8,9], improving the charge transfer kinetics using three-dimensional (3D) TiO2 semiconductors [10,11], suppression of charge recombination at photoanode/electrolyte interfaces [12,13], and enhancing electrocatalytic activity of counter electrode (CE) [14]. One attractive challenge is to employ graphene in QDSSCs. Graphene has attracted considerable attention owing to its unique 2D structure, high carrier mobility, excellent electrochemical stability, and large specific surface area. Examples include, graphene-incorporated TiO2 active films [15,16], graphene/TiO2 nanocomposite electrode [17], graphene-QD sensitizer composites [18,19], and reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/Cu2S CE [20,21].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which possess one or only a few graphene sheets less than 10 nm in size, could be promising materials with the excellent physical and electrical properties of graphene, the good optical absorption and fluorescence properties [22]. Recently, to adjust the intrinsic properties, many heteroatom-doped GQDs (e.g., B, N, F, and S) have been reported in diverse applications such as sensors, supercapacitors, and electro/photocatalysts [23–26]. Among them, the nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) have the appreciable potential for photovoltaic devices, thanks to its good photoabsorption and favorable band alignment [26–28]. Up till now, the N-GQDs have mainly been used as a single sensitizer in QDSSCs, such as N-GQD-sensitized ZnO nanorod photoanodes [29] and ozone-treated N-GQDs-sensitized solar cells [30]. On the other hand, they have more feasible approaches to boost the performance of QDSSCs, as reported in many dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) researches. Jahantigh et al. [31] studied the effect of co-sensitization in DSSCs using N-GQDs together with N719 dye, resulting in the improved power conversion efficiency (PCE). Kundu et al. [32] designed the modified TiO2 photoanode using doped GQDs to enhance the efficiency of DSSCs by its rapid electron transport and suppressed recombination rate.

Herein, we investigated the optical, electronic, and photovoltaic properties of the TiO2 mesoporous films incorporated with N-GQDs for high-performance CdSe-based QDSSCs, where the CdSe QDs were deposited onto the N-GQDs-modified TiO2 films using a successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) technique. The N-GQDs serve multiple functions, such as a co-sensitizer for MEG, providing more nucleation sites for CdSe QDs growth, enhancing the electron transport by offering an electron pathways in the TiO2 network, and suppressing charge recombination at the TiO2/electrolyte interfaces by a passivation effect. The N-GQDs-modified photoanode exhibited much higher light-harvesting and a longer electron lifetime than the photoanode without N-GQDs, resulting in a significantly enhanced short-circuit current density (Jsc) of 18.80 mA cm−2 and a PCE of 4.88% when used in QDSSCs.

Graphite powder, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, 30%), cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O), selenium dioxide (SeO2), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), copper nitrate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), boric acid (H3BO3), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O), sulfur, and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was obtained from Duksan (Korea). The solvents (acetone, ethanol, and methanol) were supplied by Daejung (Korea). All the chemicals were used without any further purification.

The N-GQDs were synthesized from graphene oxide (GO) using a hydrothermal method. The GO was synthesized using a modified Hummers method, and the experimental details are given in the supplementary. A 0.2 g sample of GO and 0.2 g of urea were well-dispersed in 40 mL of DI water and sonicated for 30 min. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 8 with NaOH. The suspension was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (100 mL) and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was filtered through a 0.20 µm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane filter and centrifuged at 8000 rpm to remove the large precipitates. Finally, blue fluorescent N-GQDs were obtained.

The FTO substrates were cleaned first in acetone, ethanol, and deionized (DI) water using a sonicator for 30 min each. TiO2 mesoporous films (~10 µm thick) were prepared by the doctor blade technique using a TiO2 paste (T/SP Ti-Nanoxide, Solaronix) on FTO substrates, and then dried at 70 °C and sintered at 450 °C for 30 min each. The active area of the TiO2 films were 0.16 cm2. The prepared TiO2 electrodes were immersed into the N-GQDs ethanol solution (0.5 mg/mL) for 4 h. Subsequently, the treated electrodes were annealed at 150 °C for 10 min in an electric furnace to form chemical bonds. CdSe QDs were grown on the N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 films using the SILAR technique. First, the FTO/TiO2/N-GQDs electrodes were immersed into a 30 mM Cd(NO3)2 ethanol solution for 1 min and rinsed with ethanol. The electrodes were then immersed into a Na2Se ethanol solution containing 30 mM SeO2 and 60 mM NaBH4 for 1 min and rinsed with ethanol. This procedure was repeated from 6 to 9 times. Finally, a ZnS passivation layer was deposited on the TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs films using the SILAR method. The electrodes were alternately immersed into an ethanolic solution of 0.1 M Zn(NO3)2 and 0.1 M Na2S mixed solution (methanol/DI water, 1:1 by volume) for 1 min each. This procedure was repeated twice to complete the photoanode preparation.
For the comparison, an FTO/TiO2/CdSe QDs photoanode was also prepared without N-GQDs modification.

The FTO/Cu2S CE was fabricated using electrodeposition (ED) followed by a sulfurization method. The Cu particles were electrochemically deposited onto the FTO substrates by ED. A mixed aqueous solution containing 5 mM CuSO4, 0.5 M H3BO3, and 1 M Na2SO4 was employed as the deposition electrolyte. Potentiostatic ED was carried out at a −0.4 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for 20 min using an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and Pt mesh counter electrode. Subsequently, the FTO/Cu films were rinsed with DI water to remove residual ions and immersed into a polysulfide aqueous solution containing 1 M Na2S and 1 M S for 30 s to transform the Cu to Cu2S, followed by rinsing and drying.

The prepared photoanode and FTO/Cu2S CE were sandwiched and sealed using a 60 µm-thick Surlyn (sealant). The electrolyte containing 2 M Na2S, 2 M S, and 0.2 M KCl in a mixture of methanol and DI water (3:7 by volume) was injected between two electrodes through a pre-drilled holes.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed to study the morphology, lattice structure, and size of the N-GQDs using a TecnaiTMG2F30 S-Twin (FEI, Netherlands) at a 300 kV accelerating voltage. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was conducted using a Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Scientific, USA) with attenuated total reflectance (ATR) technique. The Raman spectra were recorded using an XploRA PLUS (Horiba, Japan) equipped with a 532 nm laser. The optical properties of the prepared N-GQDs and photoanodes were characterized by photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) and UV–Vis spectroscopy. The PL spectra were collected using a FP-6500 (Jasco, Japan) and the UV–Vis absorbance spectra were obtained using a S-3100 (Scinco, Korea) with a wavelength resolution of 1 nm.

The chemical composition of the N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 film was characterized by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDX) coupled with a scanning electron microscope (S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out using a K-alpha (Thermo Scientific, USA) equipped with a monochromatic Al
K
α
(1487 eV) X-ray source and 180° double-focusing hemispherical analyzer. XPS spectra were recorded in constant analyzer energy (CAE) mode at a pressure of 10−8 mbar and an X-ray spot size of 400 µm. Survey spectra were obtained at a pass energy of 150 eV with a step size of 1.0 eV. The high-resolution core-level spectra were collected at a 30 eV pass energy with a 0.1 eV step size. The XPS spectra were analyzed using the CasaXPS software. The C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Ti 2p lines were fitted with a GL(70) line-shape (70% Gaussian, 30% Lorentzian).

The photocurrent density–voltage (J–V) characteristics and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the QDSSCs were measured using an IviumStat (Ivium Technologies, Netherlands). A solar simulator (PEC-L12, Peccell Technologies, Japan) equipped with a 150 W Xenon lamp was used as the light source (AM 1.5 G, 100 mWcm−2). EIS was performed over the frequency range, 10−1 to 105 Hz with an amplitude of 10 mV at the open-circuit potential. The EIS results were fitted and analyzed using the Z-View 2 software. The Mott-Schottky plots were measured from the capacitance collected from the EIS spectra at each potential with a frequency of 1000 Hz in the dark.

The electron diffusion coefficients and electron lifetimes in the TiO2 photoanode were estimated using pulse laser-induced photocurrent and voltage measurements (PLIM). The transient was induced using a Nd:YAG laser (532 nm, 10 ns-pulse) with the white light solar simulator (ABET technologies, USA) as a continuous background illumination source. The pulsed light intensities were controlled using an ND filter, and the photocurrent and voltage were recorded using an MDO-4104C oscilloscope (Tektronix, USA). The incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) analyses were carried out using a PEC-S20 (Peccell Technologies, Japan) between 300 and 900 nm with a step size of 5 nm.

The morphology of the hydrothermally synthesized N-GQDs was characterized by TEM. Fig. 1(a) shows a TEM image of the N-GQDs. Fig. 1(b) shows an HRTEM image of N-GQD with a clear lattice fringe, indicating good crystallinity. The lattice spacing distance was 0.212 nm, which was assigned to the (102) plane of graphene [33,34]. Furthermore, the absence of the (002) facet of graphite indicates that the N-GQDs were composed of only a few layers of graphene sheets [22]. Fig. 1(c) shows the measured size distribution of the N-GQDs, the average diameter was 6.988 nm. Fig. 1(d) presents the FT-IR spectra of GO and N-GQD. The broad absorption peak at 3600–3000 cm−1 was assigned to the O‒H and N‒H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1722 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 were associated with the C═O stretching vibrations in COOH and CONH, respectively [35,36]. The bands at 1594 cm−1 and 1402 cm−1 were corresponding to C═C and C─N bending vibrations, respectively, and the C─O (carboxyl) deformation peak was observed at 1350 cm−1 [37]. The peaks at 1108 cm−1 and 1012 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration in of C─O (alkoxy) and C─N, respectively [36,38]. Raman spectroscopy was performed to identify the effects of nitrogen doping. Fig. 1(e) shows the Raman spectra of N-doped GQDs and undoped GQDs, in which the undoped GQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method without adding urea. Both quantum dots showed the D (disorder) band at 1350 cm−1 and G (graphitic) band at 1603 cm−1, respectively. The D band was attributed to the presence of structural defects within the sp2 domains and the G band was assigned to the first-order scattering of E2 g mode [39]. In particular, the ratio of the intensities of the two bands (ID/IG) reflects the degree of disorder. The higher ID/IG value of the N-GQDs (1.65) than the GQDs (1.41) suggests that doping with N atoms induced numerous structural defects within the graphitic carbon [40]. Fig. 1(f) shows the UV–Vis optical absorption and PL spectra with different excitation. The inset figure shows a photograph of a diluted N-GQDs aqueous solution. The obvious absorption peak at 370 nm was attributed to the n → π* transition of the C─N/C═N conjugated structure induced by N doping, while the peak at 272 nm corresponded to the π → π* transition of the C═C aromatic sp2 domains [41,42]. The N-GQDs emit bright blue luminescence (445 nm) when excited at 380 nm and exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior with an excitation range of 320–460 nm. The PL peak shifted to longer wavelengths as the excitation wavelength was increased. This phenomenon was widely reported in carbon-based fluorescent materials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and GQDs, because of the heterogeneities in the functional groups, particle size, and surface defect states [35,43,44]. To examine the optical bandgap energy of the N-GQD, Tauc plots were investigated. The Tauc plot is a powerful and simple tool for identifying the optical bandgap of the semiconductors [45]. The bandgap can be estimated by plotting

where α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck’s constant, ν is the photon frequency, A is a proportionality constant, and
E

is the bandgap energy [46]. The exponent n depends on the nature of the electronic transition and takes a value 1/2 for direct allowed transitions. The estimated optical bandgap of N-GQD was 3.21 eV. The new energy state and the transition introduced by doping GQDs with an electron-rich hetero atom (N) modulate the electrical and optical properties and decrease the bandgap, leading to the efficient utilization of N-GQDs in a photoanode [47,48].

Fig. 2 shows SEM/EDX images of the N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 films. Owing to their small size (~7 nm), it was difficult to observe the N-GQDs on the TiO2 layer in a cross-sectional SEM image, as shown in Fig. 2(a). On the other hand, the carbon elemental mapping image (Fig. 2c) confirmed that the C atoms were dispersed uniformly throughout the TiO2 films, suggesting that the N-GQDs penetrate the mesoporous TiO2 films and are adsorbed physicochemically. For the comparison, the elemental mapping images of the bare TiO2 films before the deposition of N-GQDs was also displayed in Fig. S2. The less carbon species in bare TiO2 films (Fig. S2c) confirm that the C atoms in Fig. 2(c) were not presented from the organic residues or adsorbed contamination. These N-GQDs may provide more adsorption sites for light absorbers, such as CdSe QDs. In addition, N-GQDs may act as a co-sensitizer by its non-zero optical bandgap, enhancing the light-harvesting abilities. Fig. 2(e) presents the EDX spectrum, which confirms the presence of C, N, Ti, O, and Sn from the N-GQDs, TiO2, and FTO substrate.

To examine the surface chemical states and composition of the N-GQDs-decorated TiO2 films, XPS was carried out. Fig. 3(a) shows the survey scan spectrum with four elements: 529.39, 458.19, 399.47, and 284.37 eV, which correspond to O 1s, Ti 2p, N 1s and C 1s, respectively. The XPS fitting parameters and relative atomic percentages were determined from the survey spectrum and are listed in Table 1. The calculated nitrogen/carbon (N/C) ratio was 10.8%, suggesting that N had been introduced successfully in N-GQDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) was deconvoluted into six peaks at 288.0, 287.4, 286.4, 285.7, 284.7, and 284.2 eV, which correspond to C═O, C═N, C‒O, C‒N, C‒C, and C═C, respectively [40,49,50]. The three deconvoluted peaks of N (Fig. 3c) at 401.5, 399.9, and 399.2 eV were assigned to the graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N, respectively [28,48]. The C 1s and N 1s spectra clearly show that the N atoms bond covalently to the C atoms to form N-GQDs. The O 1s spectrum was also deconvoluted into four peaks, as shown in Fig. 3(d). The components at 532.3, 531.5, 530.7, and 529.4 eV were attributed to the C‒O‒C or C‒OH, Ti‒O‒C, C═O, and Ti‒O‒Ti, respectively [51]. The presence of Ti‒O‒C bond indicates that the N-GQDs are connected to oxygen vacant sites on the TiO2 surface by forming covalent bonds. The Ti‒O‒C bonds help enhance electron-hole separation and interfacial charge transfer [49,52]. Fig. S3 presents the core-level spectra of Ti 2p.

The PL spectra of bare N-GQDs and N-GQDs-incorporated TiO2 were displayed in Fig. S4(a). To adjust the amount of N-GQDs equally, 0.1 mL of N-GQDs solution were dropped on the FTO substrate and TiO2 films, respectively. The N-GQDs on the FTO exhibit strong fluorescence in the blue range with a peak of 446 nm under 320 nm excitation. Interestingly, PL quenching was observed in the TiO2/N-GQDs. Quenching of the fluorescence intensity shows efficient energy transfer from the N-GQDs to TiO2, indicating that the conduction band (CB) edge of the N-GQDs is located at higher than that of TiO2, and the photo-excited electrons are easily transferred to TiO2 collecting electrode [52,53]. Fig. S4(b) presents a schematic diagram of the PL quenching mechanism of the TiO2/N-GQDs system. These results show that N-GQDs can generate excitons and inject electrons into the CB of TiO2, thereby enhancing the light-harvesting and photovoltaic performance.

Fig. 4(a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the different photoanodes. The black line refers to the un-modified electrode with 8 SILAR cycles, which were optimized for the CdSe QDs, and the colored lines denote the N-GQDs-modified photoanodes with different CdSe SILAR cycles from 6 to 9. All the TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs samples exhibited a significantly higher intensity than the bare CdSe QDs sample, and their intensities increased progressively with increasing number of SILAR cycles. In the SILAR process, the QDs grow at the nucleation sites on the substrate [54]. The higher adsorption intensity of CdSe QDs on the N-GQDs-modified TiO2 than in the un-modified TiO2 indicates the higher loading of QDs. This is because the N-GQDs provide more adsorption sites with a high surface area [15]. The increase in the number of SILAR cycles allows the CdSe QD to grow larger, leading to a broadening of the absorption wavelength. Table 2 lists the estimated optical bandgap energies of TiO2/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) and TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (6,7,8 and 9 cycles). The bandgap of TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) was smaller than that of the TiO2/CdSe QDs (8 cycles), despite the same number of SILAR cycles. This indicates that the electrostatic interaction between the charged surface of N-GQDs provided by oxygen functional groups and precursor ions affected the growth of QDs, leading to larger CdSe QDs [54,55]. Therefore, the N-GQDs enhance light absorption of the photoanode by increasing the loading of CdSe QDs and stimulating CdSe QDs growth.

Fig. 5 shows the photovoltaic current density-voltage (J-V) characteristics of QDSSCs with different photoanodes and the detailed parameters are summarized in Table 3. The solar cell device with the CdSe QDs (8 cycles) photoanode exhibited a Jsc and open-circuit voltage (Voc) of 14.46 mA cm−2 and 0.520 V, respectively. Compared to this un-modified cell, all QDSSCs containing the N-GQDs showed significantly improved Jsc values, whereas Voc has decreased slightly. Among them, the cell using the TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) electrode exhibited the highest Jsc, fill factor (FF) and PCE of 18.80 mA cm−2, 54.03%, and 4.88%, respectively. Owing to the substantial improvement in Jsc, the PCE increased by approximately 24.5%, compared to the un-modified cell (3.92%). This significant improvement in Jsc can be attributed to multiple functions of N-GQDs, such as enhanced light-harvesting of QDs by providing more nucleation sites for the SILAR process and generating multiple excitons by playing the role of a co-sensitizer. Furthermore, incorporating N-GQDs can facilitate electron transport in the TiO2 film and suppress the charge recombination between injected electrons and electrolyte. The incorporation of N-GQDs decreased the Voc due to the positively shifted flat band potential of the TiO2/N-GQDs, which will be discussed later.

Pulsed laser induced photocurrent and photovoltage transient measurements (PLIMs) were carried out to identify the influence of N-GQDs incorporation on the significantly improved Jsc. These measurements are useful tools for studying the charge recombination and electron transport dynamics in a photoanode, as with intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) or intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) [56–61]. Fig. S5 shows the transient current and voltage decay curves obtained by PLIM measurements. The electron diffusion coefficient in the photoanodes was calculated by fitting the photocurrent decay curves with exp(-t

d
the fitted time constant which represents the average time when the photogenerated electrons diffuse to the collecting electrode at a short-circuit. Fig. 6(a) shows the
D
e
values plotted as a function of the current densities induced by a pulsed laser. The estimated diffusion coefficient of the cell with TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) was higher than that of the unmodified TiO2/CdSe QDs (8 cycles), indicating much faster electron transport. This is because the N-GQDs enhance the interfacial electron transfer by improving the contact between TiO2 nanoparticles and providing more electron transport paths [35,62]. In addition, the modified photoanode exhibited a noticeable increase in electron lifetime (Fig. 6b), which was estimated by transient voltage measurements. In general, the electron lifetime (
τ
n
) in the QDSSCs depends on the rate of the recombination reaction between the injected electrons and Sn 2- ions [63]. The increased electron lifetime denotes that the recombination centers (such as defects and traps) are reduced greatly. This suggests that incorporated N-GQDs passivate the centers because the formation of Ti-O-C bonds at the TiO2 surface between the N-GQDs and defects can reduce the recombination sites [49]. The charge collection efficiency (
η
col
) and diffusion length are key parameters for assessing the charge transport and recombination kinetics in the active layer. The
η
col
reflects the ratio of the number of collected electrons and the total photo-generated electrons. This can be expressed using following equation, and the calculated values are displayed in Fig. 6(c) [64].
(3)
η
col
= 1 −
τ
d
τ
n

The photoanode modified with N-GQDs exhibited
η
col
values of more than 80%, which was approximately 10% higher than the unmodified one (~70%). The higher
η
col
suggests that the electrons can be collected much more toward the external circuit. With the measurements of the diffusion coefficient and carrier lifetime, the diffusion length (
L
n
) can also be determined using the following equation [65].
(4)
L
n
=
(
D
e
τ
n
)

The diffusion length means the average distance that electrons can diffuse within their lifetime. The longer
L
n
of the N-GQDs-modified photoanode than the unmodified one suggests that the injected electrons move more efficiently through the TiO2 active layer with less annihilation (Fig. 6d). These results show that the introduction of N-GQDs in the mesoporous TiO2 can improve charge transport and suppress charge recombination, resulting in a significant increase in Jsc. Open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) measurements were also carried out to investigate the charge recombination process further (Fig. S6a). The QDSSC containing TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs showed a slower voltage decay than the cell based on the TiO2/CdSe QDs photoanode. Fig. S6(b) shows the electron lifetimes calculated from the decay curves. Similar to the results estimated using the PLIM, the N-GQDs-modified photoanode had a longer electron lifetime than the unmodified one, confirming that the N-GQDs suppress charge recombination efficiently.

EIS is a powerful tool to investigate the charge transfer kinetics. Under steady illumination, the internal resistance of the transport process of photo-generated excitons can be estimated using EIS [66]. Fig. 7 presents the Nyquist plots of the QDSSCs with various photoanodes under 1 sun illumination. The measured EIS curves were fitted by an electrical equivalent circuit (inset figure) and the resulting resistances are summarized in Table 4. The series resistance (Rs) reflects the sheet resistance of FTO and the contact resistance between the conducting substrate and active layer [67]. The first small semicircle in the high-frequency range was attributed to charge transfer resistance (R1) between Cu2S (counter electrode) and the electrolyte. The other semicircle in the low-frequency region is related directly to the charge transfer resistance (R2) at the TiO2/QDs/electrolyte interface [68]. The Rs and R1 values were similar for the samples because the same substrate and CE were used. In contrast, N-GQDs-containing QDSSCs exhibited much lower R2 values (71.5, 54.1, 41.0, and 49.3 Ω for 6, 7, 8, and 9 CdSe SILAR cycles, respectively) than that of the unmodified reference cell (82.5 Ω), suggesting that the N-GQDs enable more efficient charge transport. Among them, TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) photoanode-based QDSSCs showed the lowest R2 value, which contributed to the significantly enhanced photovoltaic performance.

Mott-Schottky (MS) measurements were carried out to analyze the reduction of Voc in the QDSSCs decorated with N-GQDs. Fig. 8 shows the MS plots of bare TiO2 and TiO2/N-GQDs electrodes. Both electrodes exhibited the positive slope of the curve, which is a characteristic of n-type semiconductors. The flat band potentials (Vfb) of each sample were estimated by extrapolation using the following equation [69,70]:
(5)
1
C
2
=
2
e
N
D
ε
ε
0
V −
V
fb
−
KT
e
where e is the electronic charge,
N
D
is the charge carrier density, ε is the relative permittivity of semiconductor,
ε
0
is the permittivity of a vacuum, V is the applied potential, K is the Boltzmann constant, and T is the absolute temperature. The Vfb of the bare TiO2 electrode was − 0.64 V vs. Ag/AgCl. On the other hand, the flat band potential was shifted slightly in the positive direction after the decoration of N-GQDs (−0.58 V vs. Ag/AgCl), leading to a lower Fermi level of the TiO2/N-GQDs and a reduced Voc. The positively shifted Vfb can be caused by the heterojunction formed at the TiO2/N-GQDs interface because of the low Fermi level of N-GQD [71]. This can also be attributed to the bandgap narrowing of TiO2 by Ti‒O‒C bond-induced energy states between the conduction and valence bands [49]. The slopes of MS curves related to the charge carrier density were similar for the two samples.

IPCE was measured for the QDSSCs modified with N-GQDs, compared to unmodified one (Fig. S7). The QDSSC with the unmodified TiO2/CdSe QDs showed a broad spectra from 300 nm to 650 nm with a long tail up to 750 nm. Compared to the unmodified TiO2/CdSe QDs, the QDSSCs with the TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs exhibited a significantly enhanced IPCE values in the overall wavelength range. The IPCE results were in a good agreement with the UV-Vis absorption spectra and J-V performances. Significantly enhanced IPCE of the optimized cell with the TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) is due to charge transport effectively enhanced by the incorporated N-GQDs, the reduced charge recombination rate and the increased IPCE on-set wavelength. Furthermore, the QDSSCs with the N-GQDs exhibited the enhanced IPCE values in the wavelength region below 400 nm, confirming that the N-GQDs function as the co-sensitizer.

Hydrothermally synthesized N-GQDs serve multiple functions in the TiO2 photoanode, leading to improved photovoltaic performance of QDSSCs. With the incorporation of N-GQDs into the mesoporous TiO2, the light-harvesting was enhanced by N-GQDs providing more nucleation sites for the CdSe QDs and acting as a co-sensitizer with a non-zero bandgap to generate more electron-hole pairs. Moreover, the TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs-based QDSSCs exhibited higher
η
col
and
L
n
than the unmodified QDSSC. This is because of the superior electronic properties, such as fast electron transport and long electron lifetime, which suppress the charge recombination rate. Therefore, QDSSCs consisting of an N-GQDs-modified photoanode with the optimized CdSe QDs (8 SILAR cycles) showed a significantly higher Jsc (18.80 mA cm−2), than that with the unmodified photoanode (14.46 mA cm−2). Although the QDSSC with the N-GQDs lowered the Voc due to the positive shift in the flat band potential, the resulting QDSSC with the TiO2/N-GQD/CdSe QDs (8 cycles) photoanode achieved significantly improved PCE (4.88%), compared to that without N-GQDs (3.92%). The incorporation of N-GQDs into nanostructured films is promising for electrodes in diverse applications, such as batteries, electrocatalysis, and photoelectrochemical water-splitting cells.
